questions.question:
Indian currency notes show the denomination indicated in at least seventeen languages. If this is not an indication of the nation's diversity, nothing else is.

Which of the following can be logically inferred from the above sentences?

A. India is a country of exactly seventeen languages.
B. Linguistic plurality is the only indicator of a nation's diversity.
C. Indian currency notes have the denomination in all their languages.
D. Linguistic plurality is strong evidence of India's diversity.
questions.answer: D. Linguistic plurality is strong evidence of India's diversity.
questions.to_find: which statement is a valid logical inference from the given argument

understand.plain: The passage says currency notes show denominations in 'at least seventeen' languages, then asserts this is a sign of diversity. The second sentence uses a rhetorical form: 'If X is not evidence of Y, nothing is,' which strongly affirms X as evidence of Y. We need to find what can be correctly inferred without overstating or distorting the claim.

keywords.explain: A lower bound — the actual number could be more than seventeen; it definitely is not exactly seventeen.
keywords.example: Saying a bag has at least 5 apples means it could have 7 or 10, but not 4.

keywords.explain: The presence of multiple languages in a society; here illustrated by currency notes displaying many languages.
keywords.example: Switzerland prints documents in four national languages, showing linguistic plurality.

keywords.explain: The phrase 'if this is not X, nothing is' is a strong rhetorical assertion meaning 'this is definitely X'.
keywords.example: 'If hard work is not a virtue, nothing is' means hard work IS a virtue.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure verbal reasoning question.

given.aim: identify the valid logical inference from a short argumentative passage

terms.example: Indian rupee notes actually show 17 regional language denominations on the language panel
terms.connects: rules out option A which claims 'exactly seventeen'

terms.meaning: the passage claims having many languages on currency IS evidence of diversity
terms.example: A multilingual currency is a concrete manifestation of the nation's linguistic heterogeneity

terms.term: rhetorical structure 'if not X, nothing is'
terms.meaning: this idiom affirms X strongly — it does not say X is the ONLY indicator
terms.example: Saying 'if honesty is not a virtue, nothing is' does not deny other virtues
terms.connects: rules out option B which claims linguistic plurality is the ONLY indicator

given.plan: Test each option against the premises. A misreads 'at least 17' as exactly 17. B adds the unjustified word 'only'. C makes a false universal claim about all languages. D is a direct, non-distorting paraphrase of the passage's conclusion.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: The text says 'at least seventeen', which is a minimum bound, not an exact count. Option A says 'exactly seventeen'. This contradicts the premise.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: The passage only says linguistic plurality IS an indicator of diversity; it does not claim it is the ONLY indicator. Inserting 'only' overstates the argument.
steps.note: B is incorrect — 'only indicator' is not supported.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: The text says notes show denomination 'in at least seventeen languages', not 'all their languages'. India has hundreds of languages; C makes an unsupported leap.
steps.note: C is incorrect — 'all their languages' is not stated.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: The rhetorical claim 'if this is not an indication of the nation's diversity, nothing else is' directly and strongly asserts that linguistic plurality on currency IS an indicator (strong evidence) of diversity. Option D paraphrases this faithfully.
steps.note: D is the correct inference — matches the passage's central assertion.

solution.result: Option D: Linguistic plurality is strong evidence of India's diversity.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal reasoning question; logical inference rules (implication, vacuous truth) are applied informally rather than through algebra or explicit formula IDs.

questions.question:
"We lived in a culture that denied any merit to literary works, considering them important only when they were handmaidens to something seemingly more urgent — namely ideology. This was a country where all gestures, even the most private, were interpreted in political terms."

The author's belief that ideology is not as important as literature is revealed by the word:

A. 'culture'
B. 'seemingly'
C. 'urgent'
D. 'political'
questions.to_find: which single word reveals that the author believes ideology is not as important as literature

understand.plain: The author describes a society that placed literature subordinate to ideology, treating ideology as the more urgent concern. The key is to find the word that signals the author's own skepticism toward this arrangement — meaning the word that qualifies or undercuts the claim that ideology is genuinely more important. The word 'seemingly' does exactly this: it implies the urgency of ideology was only apparent, not real.

keywords.explain: An adverb meaning 'appearing to be the case but perhaps not really so'; it introduces epistemic doubt.
keywords.example: 'The problem was seemingly simple' means it looked simple but might not be.

keywords.explain: A subordinate helper; calling literature a handmaiden to ideology means literature was viewed as serving ideology.
keywords.example: Using the word 'handmaiden' shows the degraded position literature was placed in, which the author resents.

keywords.explain: The author disagrees with the culture described; identifying hedging or scare-quote language locates the dissent.
keywords.example: When a writer uses 'supposedly' or 'seemingly', they are distancing themselves from the claim.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a verbal tone and word-choice analysis question.

given.aim: identify the word that encodes the author's disagreement with privileging ideology over literature

terms.meaning: the culture refused to value literary works for their own sake
terms.example: A regime that bans novels unless they promote party doctrine denies literary merit
terms.connects: establishes the author's critical view of the culture

terms.meaning: the adverb 'seemingly' is the author's implicit critique — the urgency was only apparent, not genuine
terms.example: Inserting 'seemingly' before 'more urgent' signals the author does not accept that ideology is truly more urgent
terms.connects: directly answers the question — B is correct

terms.term: all gestures interpreted in political terms
terms.meaning: a totalizing political atmosphere where even private acts become ideological
terms.example: In many 20th-century states, reading a foreign novel could be considered a political act
terms.connects: reinforces the repressive cultural backdrop the author critiques

given.plan: Scan each option word: 'culture' is neutral description; 'urgent' is part of the claim the author challenges, not the challenge itself; 'political' describes the atmosphere without qualification. Only 'seemingly' grammatically qualifies 'more urgent', signaling the author's personal skepticism.

steps.title: Identify the author's evaluative language
steps.apply: The passage describes a culture the author lived in and clearly disapproves of. The author must have embedded a qualifying word that shows his/her own judgment differs from the culture's judgment.
steps.note: Look for hedging or distancing language.

steps.title: Test option A — 'culture'
steps.apply: 'Culture' is a neutral noun labeling the context. It does not carry any evaluative charge about the importance of ideology versus literature.

steps.title: Test option C — 'urgent'
steps.apply: 'Urgent' on its own would endorse ideology's priority. But it is modified by 'seemingly', so 'urgent' alone is not the revealing word.
steps.note: C is incorrect; 'urgent' states the claim rather than qualifying it.

steps.title: Test option D — 'political'
steps.apply: 'Political' describes the mode of interpretation in the culture. It is descriptive, not evaluative of ideology's real vs. apparent importance.

steps.title: Test option B — 'seemingly'
steps.apply: The phrase 'something seemingly more urgent' inserts the adverb 'seemingly' to flag that the urgency is the culture's perception, not the author's endorsement. By calling it only 'seemingly' urgent, the author implies ideology is not REALLY more important than literature.
steps.note: B is correct — 'seemingly' is the author's epistemic qualifier that encodes the disagreement.

solution.result: Option B: 'seemingly' — the adverb marks ideology's urgency as merely apparent, revealing the author's belief that literature holds equal or greater importance.

questions.formula_note: Verbal tone analysis; no mathematical formula applies — the reasoning uses the semantic distinction between asserted and qualified claims.

questions.question:
The police arrested four criminals — P, Q, R, and S. The criminals knew each other. They made the following statements:

- P says "Q committed the crime."
- Q says "S committed the crime."
- R says "I did not do it."
- S says "What Q said about me is false".

Assume only one of the arrested four committed the crime and only one of the statements made is true. Who committed the crime?

A. P
B. R
C. S
D. Q
questions.to_find: which of the four criminals committed the crime given exactly one statement is true

understand.plain: This is a logic puzzle with four suspects and four statements, where exactly one statement is true and exactly one person committed the crime. We test each suspect as the culprit in turn and check whether exactly one statement becomes true under that assumption. The case where exactly one statement is true identifies the actual criminal.

keywords.explain: Out of the four statements, precisely one is correct and the remaining three are false.
keywords.example: If we assume R is guilty, we count how many of the four claims are true under that assumption.

keywords.term: S says Q's claim about S is false
keywords.explain: S's statement is a second-order claim: Q claimed S did it; S denies it. If S is guilty, Q is telling the truth and S is lying (consistent). If S is innocent, Q is lying and S is telling the truth (also consistent).
keywords.example: S saying 'what Q said about me is false' is equivalent to S saying 'I did not do it'.

keywords.explain: Try each suspect as the culprit and count true statements; the valid case gives exactly one true statement.
keywords.example: In logic puzzles with constraints, exhaustive case testing is the standard method.

understand.visual_alt: No geometric figure needed; a truth-table case analysis is used.

given.aim: find the single criminal among P, Q, R, S such that exactly one of the four statements is true

terms.term: P says Q committed the crime
terms.meaning: P's statement is true iff Q is the criminal
terms.example: If Q did it, P is truthful; otherwise P is lying
terms.connects: used in every case to count true statements

terms.term: Q says S committed the crime
terms.meaning: Q's statement is true iff S is the criminal
terms.example: If S did it, Q is truthful; otherwise Q is lying
terms.connects: used in every case to count true statements

terms.term: R says I did not do it
terms.meaning: R's statement is true iff R is innocent (i.e., R is NOT the criminal)
terms.example: If R did it, R is lying; if any other person did it, R is truthful
terms.connects: critical — R's denial is true in 3 of 4 cases, making it the default true statement unless R is the culprit

given.plan: For each suspect X in {P, Q, R, S}: assume X committed the crime, determine the truth value of each of the four statements, count how many are true. Accept X only if exactly one statement is true.

steps.title: Case: P committed the crime
steps.apply: P says Q did it -> FALSE (P did it). Q says S did it -> FALSE. R says I did not do it -> TRUE (R is innocent). S says Q's claim about S is false -> TRUE (Q claimed S, which is false; so S's denial is true). That gives 2 true statements. Reject.
steps.note: 2 true statements — does not satisfy the constraint.

steps.title: Case: Q committed the crime
steps.apply: P says Q did it -> TRUE. Q says S did it -> FALSE. R says I did not do it -> TRUE (R is innocent). S says Q's claim is false -> FALSE (Q correctly named S... wait: Q said S did it, but Q is the criminal, so Q's claim is false; S is correctly calling it false -> TRUE). That gives 3 true statements. Reject.
steps.note: 3 true statements — does not satisfy the constraint.

steps.title: Case: R committed the crime
steps.apply: P says Q did it -> FALSE (R did it). Q says S did it -> FALSE. R says I did not do it -> FALSE (R is the criminal; this is a lie). S says Q's claim about S is false -> TRUE (Q said S did it, but actually R did it, so Q's claim IS false; S correctly calls it false). Count of true statements: exactly 1 (S's statement). Accept.
steps.note: Exactly 1 true statement — constraint satisfied. R is the criminal.

steps.title: Case: S committed the crime (verification)
steps.apply: P says Q did it -> FALSE. Q says S did it -> TRUE. R says I did not do it -> TRUE. S says Q's claim is false -> FALSE (Q correctly said S did it, so S's denial is a lie). Count: 2 true statements. Reject.
steps.note: 2 true statements — does not satisfy the constraint.

solution.result: R committed the crime. Under this assumption, only S's statement ('what Q said about me is false') is true, satisfying the condition of exactly one true statement.

questions.formula_note: The solution applies propositional truth evaluation systematically: each statement is assigned TRUE or FALSE by substituting the assumed culprit, and the unique case with exactly one TRUE statement identifies the criminal.

questions.question:
"A recent High Court judgement has sought to dispel the idea of begging as a disease — which leads to its stigmatization and criminalization — and to regard it as a symptom. The underlying disease is the failure of the state to protect citizens who fall through the social security net."

Which one of the following statements can be inferred from the given passage?

A. Beggars are lazy people who beg because they are unwilling to work.
B. Beggars are created because of the lack of social welfare schemes.
C. Begging is an offence that has to be dealt with firmly.
D. Begging has to be banned because it adversely affects the welfare of the state.
questions.answer: B. Beggars are created because of the lack of social welfare schemes.
questions.to_find: which statement is a valid inference from the High Court passage about begging

understand.plain: The passage reframes begging: instead of treating it as a disease (something inherent to the beggar), the Court views it as a symptom of a deeper disease — the state's failure to protect citizens through the social safety net. We must find which option aligns with this reframing without adding unsupported claims.

keywords.explain: A disease is a primary condition; a symptom is a sign of an underlying cause. Calling begging a symptom places responsibility on the state, not the individual beggar.
keywords.example: A fever is a symptom of infection; treating only the fever without addressing the infection is incomplete.

keywords.explain: Government welfare programmes (unemployment benefits, food assistance, housing support) designed to prevent citizens from falling into extreme poverty.
keywords.example: Citizens who lose jobs may turn to begging if no safety net exists to support them.

keywords.explain: Treating begging as a disease leads society to stigmatize (shame) and criminalize (punish) beggars, which the Court seeks to end.
keywords.example: Several Indian states had anti-begging laws that the Court found to be misdirected.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a passage inference question.

given.aim: select the inference consistent with the passage's argument that begging results from state failure to maintain social welfare

terms.meaning: begging arises because of an external systemic failure, not an inherent defect in the beggar
terms.example: A person may beg not out of laziness but because they lost a job and found no government support
terms.connects: directly contradicts option A (laziness) and D (ban begging)

terms.term: state failure to protect through social security
terms.meaning: the root cause is inadequate welfare — absence of schemes that prevent extreme poverty
terms.example: Where social security is absent, people fall through the net and resort to begging

terms.meaning: the Court argues against treating begging as a crime or moral failing
terms.example: The ruling would oppose policies that fine or jail beggars
terms.connects: directly contradicts option C (begging is an offence to be dealt with firmly)

given.plan: Match each option against the passage's central argument. A blames beggars — passage blames the state. B reflects inadequate welfare schemes — matches 'failure of social security net'. C says deal with it as an offence — passage says dispel criminalization. D says ban begging for state welfare — passage says the state already fails its citizens.

steps.title: Identify the core argument of the passage
steps.apply: The passage argues: begging is NOT a disease of the individual; it is a symptom of the state failing to provide social security. Responsibility lies with the state, not the beggar.
steps.note: Key: blame is on state failure, not on beggars' character.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: Option A blames beggars for being lazy and unwilling to work. This is the opposite of the passage's stance, which explicitly seeks to remove blame from the beggar.
steps.note: A is contradicted by the passage.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: Option B says beggars are created by a lack of social welfare schemes. This is a direct paraphrase of 'the failure of the state to protect citizens who fall through the social security net'. A missing or inadequate social security net = lack of social welfare schemes.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: Option C treats begging as an offence requiring firm action. The passage explicitly criticises the criminalization of begging and aims to dispel it.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: Option D claims banning begging benefits the state. The passage argues the state has failed its citizens; framing a ban as good for the state inverts the logic of the passage.

solution.result: Option B: Beggars are created because of the lack of social welfare schemes — this directly reflects the passage's conclusion that begging is a symptom of the state's failure to maintain the social security net.

questions.formula_note: This verbal reasoning question requires identifying the inference consistent with the passage's argument; no mathematical formulas are involved.
